metadata.title: Dekho Panchang — The Science of Indian Astronomy
metadata.description: Explore the scientific foundations of the Indian Panchang system, generate your Kundali, and receive detailed Tippanni commentary.

home.subtitle: Explore the astronomical brilliance of India's ancient calendar system — where precise celestial mechanics meet timeless wisdom
home.panchangDesc: The five elements of the Indian astronomical calendar — Tithi, Nakshatra, Yoga, Karana, and Vara — computed from precise solar and lunar positions.
home.kundaliDesc: Generate your birth chart (Janma Patrika) using the exact planetary positions at your moment of birth, computed via Swiss Ephemeris.
home.tippanniDesc: Receive comprehensive commentary on your chart — personality insights, yoga analysis, dasha predictions, and life area interpretations.
home.scienceDesc: The Indian Panchang is rooted in precise astronomical observations dating back thousands of years.
home.sciencePoint1: Unlike the Western tropical zodiac, the Indian system tracks actual stellar positions using the sidereal zodiac, accounting for the precession of equinoxes (Ayanamsha).
home.sciencePoint2: The Panchang elegantly synchronizes lunar months with the solar year through the intercalary month (Adhik Masa), a mathematical feat rivaling modern calendrical systems.
home.sciencePoint3: Ancient Indian astronomers predicted eclipses, planetary conjunctions, and celestial events with remarkable accuracy — as documented in the Surya Siddhanta (c. 400 CE).
home.exploreToolsDesc: Comprehensive Vedic astrology tools — from sign calculators to muhurat finders, eclipse calendars to devotional guides

about.title: About Dekho Panchang
about.subtitle: The science behind India's astronomical heritage

learn.title: Learn Jyotish
learn.subtitle: A beginner's journey into Vedic astronomy — from the night sky to the Panchang
learn.foundationsSubtitle: Before diving into Tithis, Nakshatras, and charts — let's understand the sky itself
learn.nightSkyContent: Step outside on a clear night. You see thousands of tiny points of light — these are stars. Among them, a few shine steadily without twinkling — these are planets (Grahas). The brightest object is the Moon (Chandra), and during the day, the Sun (Surya) dominates. Ancient Indian astronomers observed these objects for millennia, tracking their patterns with extraordinary precision. Jyotish begins here — with looking up.
learn.celestialSphereContent: Imagine the sky as a giant dome above you — a sphere surrounding the Earth. This is the 'celestial sphere.' The point directly above you is the zenith. The line where the sky meets the ground is the horizon. The celestial poles are the points around which all stars appear to rotate. This imaginary sphere is the canvas on which all of Jyotish is drawn.
learn.eclipticContent: The Sun doesn't stay in the same spot — it moves across the sky over the course of a year, tracing a great circle called the ecliptic. This path is tilted 23.5° relative to Earth's equator (because Earth's axis is tilted). The ecliptic is the fundamental reference line in Jyotish. All planets and the Moon move very close to this line, within a belt of about 8-9° on either side.
learn.degreesMeasurementContent: The ecliptic circle, like any circle, is 360°. We measure positions along this circle in degrees. When we say 'the Moon is at 45°,' we mean it's 45 degrees along the ecliptic from a fixed starting point. In Indian astronomy, this starting point is the star Spica (Chitra), and the system is called Nirayana (sidereal). Each degree can be divided into 60 minutes (arcminutes), and each minute into 60 seconds (arcseconds).
learn.zodiacBeltContent: The zodiac is a belt extending about 9° on each side of the ecliptic — an 18° wide band. This belt is divided into 12 equal parts of 30° each. These are the 12 Rashis (signs). The Sun takes roughly one month to traverse each Rashi. The Moon takes about 2.25 days per Rashi. This 12-fold division is the backbone of astrological measurement.
learn.tropicalVsSiderealContent: Here's where Indian and Western astronomy diverge. Due to a wobble in Earth's axis (precession), the equinox point slowly shifts backward through the zodiac — about 1° every 72 years. Western (tropical) astrology ignores this and defines 0° Aries as the spring equinox point, which moves. Indian (sidereal/Nirayana) astrology fixes 0° Aries to the actual star Spica minus 180°. The difference between these two systems is called Ayanamsha — currently about 24°. This means your 'Sun sign' differs between Western and Indian systems.
learn.sunsJourneyContent: The Sun completes one full circle of the ecliptic in approximately 365.25 days — the solar year. When the Sun crosses from south to north of the celestial equator, it's Uttarayana (northward journey) — considered auspicious. The reverse is Dakshinayana. Each crossing into a new Rashi is called Sankranti. The Sun's exact position (solar longitude) is the anchor for many Panchang calculations.
learn.moonsJourneyContent: The Moon orbits Earth in about 27.3 days (sidereal month) but takes 29.5 days to go from New Moon to New Moon (synodic month). This difference exists because Earth is also moving around the Sun. In Jyotish, the Moon is the most important body — even more than the Sun. The Moon's position determines your Nakshatra, your Rashi for predictive purposes, and drives Tithi and Yoga calculations. The Moon moves about 13° per day — the fastest of all visible celestial bodies.
learn.fiveLimbsContent: The word 'Panchang' means 'five limbs' — five astronomical elements calculated from the positions of the Sun and Moon. These are: (1) Tithi — based on the angular distance between Moon and Sun, (2) Nakshatra — the Moon's position among 27 stellar divisions, (3) Yoga — the combined longitude of Sun and Moon, (4) Karana — half a Tithi, and (5) Vara — the weekday. Together, these five elements describe the astronomical signature of any moment in time. Each is explored in detail in the tabs above.
learn.grahasSubtitle: The nine astronomical bodies that form the foundation of Vedic astrology
learn.grahasWhat: In Jyotish, 'Graha' doesn't just mean 'planet' — it means 'that which grasps or influences.' The Navagraha (nine Grahas) include the Sun, Moon, five visible planets (Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn), and two mathematical points where the Moon's orbit crosses the ecliptic (Rahu and Ketu). These nine entities form the basis of all astrological analysis.
learn.grahasAstronomy: The Sun and Moon are luminaries. Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn are the five planets visible to the naked eye, known since antiquity. Rahu and Ketu are the ascending and descending lunar nodes — the points where the Moon's orbital plane intersects the ecliptic. Eclipses occur when the Sun and Moon are near these nodes.
learn.rashisTitle: Rashis — The 12 Signs of the Zodiac
learn.rashisSubtitle: The twelve 30° divisions of the ecliptic belt
learn.rashisWhat: A Rashi (zodiac sign) is one of 12 equal divisions of the ecliptic, each spanning exactly 30°. Unlike Western astrology which uses the tropical zodiac, Jyotish uses the sidereal zodiac — aligned with actual star positions. Each Rashi has a ruling planet, an element (fire, earth, air, water), and a quality (cardinal, fixed, dual).
learn.rashisAstronomy: The 360° ecliptic is divided into 12 segments of 30° each, starting from 0° Aries (Mesha). A planet's Rashi is determined by which 30° segment its sidereal longitude falls in. For example, if the Moon is at 47°, that's 47 - 30 = 17° into the second sign (Taurus/Vrishabha).
learn.nakshatrasSubtitle: The Moon's journey through 27 star clusters along the ecliptic
learn.nakshatrasWhat: Nakshatras are 27 equal divisions of the ecliptic, each spanning 13°20' (13.333°). They represent the Moon's daily resting places as it circles the sky in ~27 days. Each Nakshatra is identified with a star or star group, has a ruling deity, a planetary lord, and a distinctive nature. The Nakshatra system is uniquely Indian and predates the Rashi system.
learn.nakshatrasAstronomy: The sidereal zodiac (360°) divided by 27 gives 13°20' per Nakshatra. The Moon's Nakshatra is found by: Nakshatra number = floor(Moon's sidereal longitude / 13.333) + 1. Each Nakshatra is further divided into 4 Padas (quarters) of 3°20' each. The Moon takes approximately 1 day per Nakshatra.
learn.tithisSubtitle: The angular relationship between Moon and Sun defines each lunar day
learn.tithisWhat: A Tithi is one of 30 divisions of the lunar month, determined by the angular distance between the Moon and the Sun. Each Tithi spans exactly 12° of elongation. The 30 Tithis are divided into two Pakshas: Shukla (waxing, Tithis 1-15) and Krishna (waning, Tithis 1-15). Tithi is the most important Panchang element for determining festival dates and auspicious timings.
learn.tithisAstronomy: Tithi = floor((Moon longitude - Sun longitude) / 12°) + 1. When the Moon-Sun difference is 0-12°, it's Shukla Pratipada (first Tithi). At 180°, it's Purnima (Full Moon). At 360°/0°, it's Amavasya (New Moon). Since the Moon moves ~13°/day and the Sun ~1°/day, the elongation increases ~12°/day — hence one Tithi per day approximately.
learn.yogasSubtitle: The combined longitude of Sun and Moon creates 27 Yogas
learn.yogasWhat: A Yoga in the Panchang context is one of 27 divisions based on the sum of the Sun's and Moon's sidereal longitudes. Each Yoga spans 13°20' of this combined longitude. Unlike its namesake in physical practice, Panchang Yoga is purely astronomical — it reflects the combined energy of the two luminaries.
learn.yogasAstronomy: Yoga = floor((Sun longitude + Moon longitude) / 13.333°) + 1. The sum increases as both bodies move forward. Since the Moon moves much faster than the Sun (~13° vs ~1° per day), the sum increases by ~14° daily — roughly one Yoga per day. The 27 Yogas have varying qualities from highly auspicious (Siddha) to inauspicious (Vyaghata).
learn.karanasSubtitle: Each Tithi has two halves, giving 60 Karanas per lunar month from 11 types
learn.karanasWhat: A Karana is half a Tithi — spanning 6° of Moon-Sun elongation. There are 11 Karana types: 4 fixed (Shakuni, Chatushpada, Naga, Kimstughna) that occur once each per month, and 7 repeating (Bava, Balava, Kaulava, Taitila, Garaja, Vanija, Vishti) that cycle through the remaining 56 positions. Vishti (Bhadra) Karana is considered highly inauspicious.
learn.karanasAstronomy: Karana = floor((Moon longitude - Sun longitude) / 6°) + 1. There are 60 Karanas per lunar month (2 per Tithi × 30 Tithis). The 4 fixed Karanas occupy positions 1, 58, 59, and 60. The 7 repeating Karanas cycle through positions 2-57 (8 complete cycles).
learn.muhurtasSubtitle: Each day is divided into 30 Muhurtas, each governed by a deity
learn.muhurtasWhat: A Muhurta is one-thirtieth of a day (sunrise to sunrise), approximately 48 minutes. The day is split into 15 daytime Muhurtas (sunrise to sunset) and 15 nighttime Muhurtas (sunset to sunrise). Each Muhurta has a presiding deity and carries auspicious or inauspicious qualities. The 8th daytime Muhurta (Abhijit) is the most auspicious, while Muhurtas 26-27 form the sacred Brahma Muhurta.
learn.muhurtasAstronomy: Daytime Muhurta duration = (Sunset - Sunrise) / 15. Nighttime Muhurta duration = (Next Sunrise - Sunset) / 15. In summer, daytime Muhurtas are longer (~55-60 min) and nighttime shorter. In winter, the reverse. The Abhijit Muhurta falls around solar noon. Brahma Muhurta occurs roughly 96 minutes before sunrise.
learn.kundaliSubtitle: A map of the sky at the exact moment and place of birth
learn.kundaliWhat: A Kundali (Janma Patrika) is a diagram showing the positions of all nine Grahas across the 12 Rashis (houses) at the exact moment of birth, as seen from the birth location. The Lagna (Ascendant) — the Rashi rising on the eastern horizon at birth — becomes the first house. The entire chart rotates based on birth time and location, making each Kundali unique.
learn.kundaliAstronomy: The Ascendant (Lagna) is calculated from the local sidereal time at birth, which depends on the date, time, and geographic longitude. The 12 houses are derived from the Ascendant using various house division systems (most commonly equal house in Indian astrology). Each planet is placed in its Rashi based on its sidereal longitude at the birth moment.
